examples.title: Simulating CAP Trade-off Decision
examples.description: Demonstrates how a distributed system decides between consistency and availability during partition
examples.code:
FUNCTION HandleReadRequest(nodeId, dataKey)
  IF networkPartitionDetected THEN
    IF systemMode == CP_MODE THEN
      RETURN ERROR_UNAVAILABLE
    ELSE IF systemMode == AP_MODE THEN
      localData = GET_FROM_LOCAL_REPLICA(dataKey)
      RETURN localData
    END IF
  ELSE
    data = GET_FROM_PRIMARY_REPLICA(dataKey)
    SYNC_TO_SECONDARY_REPLICAS(dataKey, data)
    RETURN data
  END IF
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates how a system chooses behavior during network partition based on CP or AP mode. In CP mode, the system returns an error (unavailable) to maintain consistency. In AP mode, it returns local data which may be stale.

examples.title: Eventual Consistency Implementation
examples.description: Simple implementation of eventual consistency using write and read operations
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates eventual consistency where writes are propagated asynchronously and reads may return stale data from any replica.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for write to n replicas, O(1) for read

examples.title: Strong Consistency Implementation
examples.description: Implementation showing strong consistency with quorum-based reads and writes
examples.explanation: This Java code implements strong consistency using quorum-based approach. Both reads and writes require acknowledgment from a majority of replicas, ensuring all nodes see the same data.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of replicas
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing versions from each replica
